/collection/timecapsule.title: Time Capsule - Shannon Prendergast Portfolio
p: The briefing for Time Capsule was to create a publication based on the year 2024. The publication needed to be a sign of the times by using three chosen objects, ideas or symbols, to create a publication that was a time capsule of the year. This project shows my developed publication for Time Capsule. My three chosen themes were centered around America's political climate, specifically conspiracy theories, politicians and influencer culture.

I had to find a way to incorporate all three chosen themes harmoniously into one book, bringing attention to my topics through apparent and meaningful design choices while juggling various different projects and deadlines at the same time.

I focused on hiding conspiracy theories inside the book by using a french fold, a method of bookbinding where the fold of a page is faces outwards rather than into the spine, meaning there is hidden space inside the page. I wanted to raise the question of why a large number of America's politicians are over the age of 60. Each page number in the book is false, and is instead the age of the politician in question. I also focused on areas of the politicians faces instead of them as a whole, to bring attention to their details instead of their identity. I then had a dedicated section at the end of the book on influencer culture being perceived in the real world, showing the nature and possible absurdities of constant phone use and self surveillance in public.

I prototyped my book cover and layout through a set of sketches and selected layouts, incorporating the layout in Indesign then collecting and editing assets of my chosen media to add. Once I was happy with the layout and design I printed my pages to then bind into my book.

I became knowledgable in the entire process of creating a publication, from prototyping and designing to printing and pressing. This knowledge was valuable to have and helped me to create a more ambitious publication in my RFI project. Adding a section for conspiracy theories that was in theme with the subject matter by having hidden areas in the book for the viewer to uncover taught me that detailed and subtle pieces of information are just as impactful as big pieces.

/collection/dyson.title: Dyson - Shannon Prendergast Portfolio
p: During my three years with the agency Spicerack, I worked with Dyson from an apprentice to a designer, creating hundreds of HTML5 banner advertisements, animations, storyboards and website amendments.

Dyson needed to produce multiple standardised HTML5 adverts as well as storyboards for their upcoming projects. Due to the sheer volume of work that Dyson required due to their global reach and regular product launches, Spicerack was entrusted to work alongside Dyson's design team to help complete projects.

Our team storyboarded and animated for their upcoming international projects and helped design and update their UK/US websites. We constantly held reviews with Dyson's design team; maintaining strong communication between our design departments allowed us to streamline feedback and produce productive results under time constraints.

I worked to create HTML5 banners of varying sizes using Adobe Animate, ensuring that the animations were all consistent to the storyboards, and that animation timings remained perfect across all size variations.

I gained invaluable experience on working with larger clients and tackling a high volume of projects under strict time constraints. Working collaboratively with Dyson's internal design team improved my collaborative skills and further taught me the importance of communication. I became much more experienced in 2D animation and learnt to seamlessly navigate Adobe Animate.

/collection/aaa.title: Access All Areas - Shannon Prendergast Portfolio
p: The brief for Access All Areas was to disturb an unused space with typography. This shows my journey from concept to execution. My chosen location was the Millwall Slipway as from the 20th May 2024, Thames Water had dumped raw sewage in the River Thames for more than 1,900 hours – equivalent to 79 days. This data was collected using the sewage map website by the organisation River Action. River Action found an average of 2,869 E.coli colony forming units (CFU) per 100ml of water in the river, making it completely unsafe. I wanted my chosen type and location to bring attention to the ongoing problem with the River Thames.

I had to create a typographic intervention in a public space. The piece had to be related to the area, and I needed to consider how to effectively draw people’s attention towards the piece. I also had to decide the method I was going to use to create my first prototype.

After thorough research into the space I knew that my intervention had to be about the river itself to highlight some of the ecological problems surrounding the river. I hosted a word generation session with friends at university to pick and highlight which words were the most significant to our spaces. I chose the words "neglected waters", which I later shortened to "neglected", I designed a sculpture that relied on the river in order to be legible, mirroring the design so that it was only comprehensible when reflected in the water's surface.

I found news articles surrounding my chosen place, selected and then randomised words from these articles and then chose my desired word. From there I planned, designed and rendered my typographic intervention in Blender. I decided to use the water in my space to help make a point and accentuate my word by having my typographic intervention light up and be backwards. The actual reflection of the water would reveal the true meaning of the intervention.

Access All Areas made me consider how to bring attention to serious topics through design. Effective design in the real world can hold strong relevance to the space around it, and be a part of influencing change. I became more proficient at Blender, learning the importance of correct lighting use by using HDRI's to simulate the lighting of my real world space.

/collection/transition.title: Transition - Shannon Prendergast Portfolio
p: The brief for Transition detailed the task of creating a short animation with no words or people in, but had to show two opposing subjects. These subjects were assigned to us randomly, my two being near and far.
I needed to plan and create a visual response that was in line with my selected prompt, something that was not too literal, but still visually explainable.

I understood that the words near and far were contradictory, so I began to look into visuals that could make the viewers' perception contradict itself. I researched various illusions and camera tricks to achieve this effect and settled on the dolly zoom effect. The effect creates a disorienting feeling by zooming in while moving the camera backwards. I decided to create an animation using the same effect. To expand on the theme I designed and crafted a zoetrope, a pre-cinema animation device to bring the animation into a physical space. The rotation of the zoetrope further related to my prompts of near and far, as the animation physically works on a rotational mechanism.

I created a 3D animation on Blender, simulated the dolly zoom using the camera and then rendered the animation. I then prototyped, designed and laser cut a zoetrope to display parts of the animation on. I displayed this at an exhibition in The Ugly Duck gallery in London.

The physical is just as important as the digital. I noticed how much curiosity and excitement my zoetrope had given viewers when displayed in a physical space, showing me the importance of designing in and for physical spaces. Thinking of alternative and creative solutions to display something can be as fun for you to create as it is for the viewer to experience. I further strengthened my skills using 3D software such a Blender, and gained experience at designing, prototyping, testing and building a physical object using Adobe Illustrator and CAM.

/collection/rfi.title: RFI - Shannon Prendergast Portfolio
p: I was briefed to use editorial book design to deep dive into a subject area and convey carefully chosen information about my topic. I chose the history, aesthetics, design and rule-sets of the table top RPG Dungeons & Dragons, creating a detailed compendium of media to use and edit into the book.

I needed to undertake a lot of research in a short amount of time, and amass a large amount of content in order to fill an entire book. The task to research, prototype, design, print and bind a publication in less than four months was a big task, making it essential for me to plan my time accordingly.

I reached out to members of the Dungeons & Dragons community to talk about the stories they created through the game, as well as making connections with owners of TTRPG game spaces, pubs and bars across London. Through doing this I was able to gather a lot of unique resources by taking photos of games being played and archival game manuals.

I created a giant folder space to allocate all images and documents by their correct edition, date and/or book section. From this I could take out my desired material and begin to edit it for the book while writing the copy for each section. After I was happy with this, I printed my pages and began to bind the book, making a transparent sleeve to protect the cover whilst still displaying the design.

This project made me understand on a deeper level how layout, colour, paper, typeface and alignment play major roles in successfully communicating your piece of work to viewers. I now know how to create a large full scale editorial project with a finalised printed outcome, and how to manage such a huge amount of work effectively.

/collection/gdr.title: GDR UK - Shannon Prendergast Portfolio
p: GDR provides services to clients based on consumer insight and emerging retail trends. This project shows my work on GDR’s website alongside senior designer Chris Helingoe and front end developers at the agency Spicerack.
GDR wanted a new website that was more streamlined, adopting their new brand guidelines with a focus on improving the user experience.

We held multiple workshop sessions with the client to go over and re-create the user journey, and collaborated with the client to implement their creative branding in innovative ways throughout the website, leading to more streamlined navigation and a fresh new look.

I collaborated with the senior designer and front end developers to figure out what was feasible, taking assets from the new branding and creating new assets influenced by the guidelines. I then began prototyping and testing various formats and designs while gaining feedback both from the senior designer and client.

This was the first time during working with Spicerack that I was entrusted with leading the creative aspects of a project. This allowed me to make impactful creative decisions to influence the direction of the project, and also improved my UI design skills as I was in charge of all web pages.

/collection/passage.title: Passage - Shannon Prendergast Portfolio
p: I was briefed to create a response to a piece of text using motion design or experimental animation. Taking a piece of text from Sylvia Plath's book the bell jar, I created an experimental outcome based around the quote. I was able to achieve this outcome by using motion capture techniques, 3d modelling, geometry nodes and Blender. “I saw my life branching out before me like the green fig tree in the story. From the tip of every branch, like a fat purple fig, a wonderful future beckoned and winked. One fig was a husband and a happy home and children, and another fig was a famous poet and another fig was a brilliant professor, and another fig was Ee Gee, the amazing editor, and another fig was Europe and Africa and South America, and another fig was Constantin and Socrates and Attila and a pack of other lovers with queer names and offbeat professions, and another fig was an Olympic lady crew champion, and beyond and above these figs were many more figs I couldn't quite make out. I saw myself sitting in the crotch of this fig tree, starving to death, just because I couldn't make up my mind which of the figs I would choose. I wanted each and every one of them, but choosing one meant losing all the rest, and, as I sat there, unable to decide, the figs began to wrinkle and go black, and, one by one, they plopped to the ground at my feet.” ― Sylvia Plath, The Bell Jar
I needed to create a full animated short film to a high standard within the short time frame given, whilst still working on various personal and professional projects.

I made a new deadline ten days in advance of the formal deadline to make room for any errors or possible complications, making sure I had extra time to finish the work. I then worked backwards from my new deadline, setting smaller task specific deadlines to ensure the work was completed in time.

Based on my research of my chosen quote and the book, I storyboarded my ideas then filmed myself for motion capture. I created a 3D model using Daz 3D and began to edit, create, sculpt and render in Blender 3D, then finally merged all scenes in After Effects to export the final film.

This project taught me how crucial it is to have an impactful visual narrative if you're planning on creating a story with no written or spoken words. I also improved my ability to animate seamlessly in both 2D and 3D software.

/collection/sonic-frontiers.title: Sonic Frontiers - Shannon Prendergast Portfolio
p: Working with senior designers, developers and SEGA on the design and innovation for their email marketing.SEGA is a video game company. This page shows my work creating a newsletter for their game Sonic Frontiers.

We were asked to support SEGA's creative services team in designing, building and testing more engaging email templates for campaigns promoting their upcoming game releases and competitions. SEGA needed a newsletter for their game Sonic Frontiers to promote the game and gain traction for pre-orders.

I worked alongside our developers to create an eye catching newsletter design, using SEGA's assets and copy to re-work and create new and exciting assets, as well as implementing interactive elements within their newsletters.

I created the full newsletter design by brainstorming, designing and adding in interactive elements, such as custom GIFs made from SEGA's assets. I tailored multiple versions of the newsletter in line with specific guidelines for different countries.
I learnt how to create engaging and innovative designs by recycling SEGA's old assets and turning them into something fresh, making new work that remained consistent with their brand guidelines.

/collection/symprove.title: Symprove - Shannon Prendergast Portfolio
p: Working alongside senior designer Chris Helingoe and client Symprove as a junior designer on UX recommendations and designs.Symprove is a water-based probiotic supplement that contains live and active bacteria to support gut health. This page shows my role and the work I did re-designing their website, collaborating alongside other designers, a creative lead, developers and the client themselves.

Symprove wanted improved functionality spanning across all webpages, as well as redefining their user journey on core user pages. Symprove also wanted a new and well-defined look in match with their new brand guidelines,

We held multiple workshops and a UX audit with Symprove to re-define the user journey, before planning and implementing new approaches. This led to improvements to the website's functionality as well as streamlining the user experience.

I focused on simplifying the checkout process and optimised widgets for mobile in order to create a more intuitive and user friendly experience. I started with brainstorming and making wire-frames, then prototyping and testing various outcomes for both the website and advertisements, keeping everything in line with Symprove's design language and emerging brand standards.

Maintaining continuous communication with the clients at Symprove to update them on my progress, as well as being proactive in seeking out feedback from fellow designers and developers to identify any limitations or proposed improvements affirmed the importance of communication in large scale projects. This project led me to practice critical thinking, understanding more about a users needs and behaviours to find effective solutions as well as improving my skills in Adobe XD and Sketch.
